assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

NOTE: Ideally, you should have a notebook that is at least 8 x 10 inches, so you have room to draw your own maps and also glue in maps that are printed off.

➜ INTRO
This year, we are learning about mapmaking and global exploration. You will be learning to draw your own maps as well. You may want to first practice outlining the map on a whiteboard or another piece of paper before putting it in your notebook. As time permits on drawing days, you are encouraged to use watercolors or colored pencils to fill in your outlines (see the link for examples done by students). Your geography notebook will be used on both days of the week. Make sure you label each entry with the date or lesson number. There will be days that you will be prompted to follow the instructions for an activity in the Activity Section of your Mapping the World with Art book.
∞ Image Link: Watercolor Map Examples

➜ READ
Reading 1: The Very Earliest Maps

➜ NARRATE & NOTEBOOK
1\. Narrate orally what you remember about the reading.
2\. Then, in your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can look back at the text as needed. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw a concept map or pictures with captions. You can be as creative as you like. We provided examples of other students' work. Typically, each two-page spread in your notebook will have a narration on one side and your map drawings on the other. There may be weeks in which you will have two drawings per two-page spread (especially in Term 3).
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 1
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 2
∞ Image Link: Example Notebook Entry 3

➜ DRAW
p.1-2 Map Drawing 1 Mesopotamia (Use the book or video option.)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. For example, under the map of Mesopotamia, you could say, "The earliest maps were drawn in clay by the people of Mesopotamia."

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Mesopotamia as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Mesopotamia

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

What did you read last time about the very earliest maps?

➜ READ

2\. In your notebook, include some kind of narration. You can write a short paragraph, write bullet points, or draw diagrams or pictures with captions.

➜ DRAW
p.3-4 Map Drawing 2 Nile River (Use the book or video option.)
Be sure to include a sentence that tells how the map pertains to the reading. This map pertains to the first reading. You might have to look back to remember what was said about Egypt and the earliest maps.

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Nile River as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of the Nile River

• EXTRA HELPING
Check out this video for a deeper look at the history of cartography.
∞ Video Link: Origin of the World Map

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Mapping the World with Art
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 7 (Canada) — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Mapping the World with Art: Tell about Marco Polo and why he was important to the history of maps. OR Tell about Henry the Navigator and his contributions to map-making.

· Mapping the World with Art: Draw a map of one of the countries you learned about this term and label anything you can remember. Suggestions include the following: Italy, Greece, the Iberian Peninsula, Britain, Israel, India, Africa, and South America.

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook, parchment paper, copy of pattern on p.129, pen(s), color pencils

PREP: Make sure you have the materials needed for the map drawing activity. You may purchase parchment paper for printing from Amazon or Staples. However, if you would like to age your paper, take a look at the video link. I suggest you make the aged paper a day before you are going to use it. This will give the paper time to dry for use in the lesson.
∞ Video Link: How To Make Paper Look Old

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
p.128 Activity Section: Map review (This will take you two weeks to complete, especially if you take your time and add color to your map.)
Activity Idea 30C: Make an antique map of the Eastern Hemisphere. Follow the instructions in the book. This will be a good review of the locations you have already drawn in the Eastern Hemisphere.

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook, parchment paper, copy of pattern on p.129, pen(s), color pencils

assignment_description:
Canada Mapping
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

PREP: Print 5 copies of the linked blank map of Canada to be used over the next several weeks. One of these is to be placed in your student's geography notebook (leave an opposite page blank for notes). Information will be added from week to week, and the map will be used to study each week. The additional maps are used periodically during the term to see what the student remembers.
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of Canada (colour)
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of Canada (b/w)

➜ INTRO
One lesson each week throughout this term will be spent on Canadian geography. This includes map activities and map work, as well as reading about explorers who lived during this period in our history rotation (1800-1900).

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Before we look more closely inside Canada, we're going to start with what is outside. What types of borders does Canada have? [A: land borders and ocean borders]
2\. What country borders Canada to the south? [A: United States of America] What ocean borders Canada to the east? [A: Atlantic Ocean] What ocean borders Canada to the west? [A: Pacific Ocean] What ocean borders Canada to the north? [A: Arctic Ocean]
3\. Add the United States of America, the Atlantic Ocean, the Pacific Ocean, and the Arctic Ocean to your map.
4\. Canada also has other borders in the Arctic. What land borders Canada to the northwest? [A: a land border with Alaska, United States of America] What land borders Canada to the northeast? [A: a maritime border with Greenland (Denmark)] What body of water is north of Yukon and Alaska? [A: Beaufort Sea] What body of water is south of Yukon and Alaska? [A: Gulf of Alaska] What body of water is between Baffin Island and Greenland? [A: Baffin Bay]
5\. Add Alaska, Greenland (Denmark), Beaufort Sea, Gulf of Alaska, and Baffin Bay to your map.
6\. Tape this map into your notebook. Leave the opposite page blank for any notes about Canada as a country you decide to include during the year.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.14-31
Maps can convey information in so many different ways beyond borders and place names. Spend some time reading through the types of maps found in this section and pause on the ones that interest you the most.
• In your geography notebook, write down some facts you've learned from these maps about your local area and/or home province/territory. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.61-63 Map Drawing 26A Australia, 26B New Zealand

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at these maps of Australia and New Zealand as you draw yours.

Canada Mapping - Nunavut & Newfoundland and Labrador
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Nunavut
2\. What are the borders of Nunavut? [A: Northwest Territories, Manitoba, Hudson Bay, Davis Strait, Labrador Sea]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: Iqaluit]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Barbeau Peak - 2616 m - located in the far northern part of the territory]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Newfoundland and Labrador
2\. What are the borders of Newfoundland and Labrador? [A: Quebec, Atlantic Ocean, Labrador Sea, Gulf of St. Lawrence]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: St. John's]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Caubvick - 1652m - located in the far northwest part of Labrador on the border with Quebec]

➜ MAP WORK
You will begin to label your blank map of Canada in your geography notebook.
1\. Label Nunavut and Newfoundland & Labrador.
2\. For both, label the capital, a couple of the major bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. (Optional) If you have additional time, choose one province/territory and write at least 3 things about it using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Alaska as you draw yours.

Introduction: Robert McClure and the Northwest Passage
⍞ Materials: Exploring the Frozen North

➜ INTRO
The Arctic loomed large in nineteenth-century imaginations. At the end of the Napoleonic Wars, and after centuries of exploration, the British navy found itself with little to do besides setting off to explore the two regions still largely unexplored: the Arctic and Antarctic regions. The quest to find a Northwest Passage through the Arctic from the Atlantic to the Pacific Ocean and open a faster trade route to Asia resulted in many failed and doomed expeditions, including the Franklin Expedition that departed England in 1845.
This term, you will read Trapped in the Arctic, a short book about Robert McClure, an officer in the British Royal Navy, who captained the _Investigator_, a ship on a dual mission to search for the Franklin Expedition and to find the Northwest Passage through the Arctic.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.144-152 "September 1851" - "proper to his command."

• How much of the story of Arctic exploration is familiar to you already? Can you imagine what it would have been like to have parts of the globe that were completely unknown?

➜ VIEW
Look at the portrait of McClure, painted in 1855 by Stephen Pearce after the captain's return from the Arctic. What details do you notice?
∞ Image Link: Portrait of Sir Robert McClure

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.66-69 Map Drawing 27B Kamchatka Peninsula and the Sea of Okhotsk

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of the Kamchatka Peninsula & the Sea of Okhotsk as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Kamchatka Peninsula & the Sea of Okhotsk

A Daring Gamble
⍞ Materials: Exploring the Frozen North, Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about Robert McClure and the history of Arctic exploration from the last lesson?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.152-160 "Miertsching, a pious young evangelist" - "the ground he had gained."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.68-69
1\. Trace the route of the Investigator from Hawaii towards the Kamchatka Peninsula. Compare Collinson's route around the Aleutian Islands to McClure's route through them. Consult the map on p.155 of Exploring the Frozen North for a more detailed view of the west coast of Alaska.
2\. Add more details to your map of the Kamchatka Peninsula and the Sea of Okhotsk from the last lesson, if you'd like.

• EXTRA HELPING
McClure's voyage occurred before the International Date Line was firmly established, but his ordered route follows the line closely. Learn more about how time zones and the International Date Line work.
∞ Video Link: The longest day ever | The international date line

Canada Mapping - Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Nova Scotia
2\. What are the borders of Nova Scotia? [A: New Brunswick, Gulf of St. Lawrence, Atlantic Ocean, and also the Bay of Fundy, which is not labelled on this map]
3\. Turn to p.41. What is the capital? [A: Halifax]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: White Hill - 532m - located on Cape Breton Island]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: New Brunswick
2\. What are the borders of New Brunswick? [A: Quebec, Nova Scotia, United States of America, Gulf of St. Lawrence]
3\. Turn to p.40. What is the capital? [A: Fredericton]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Carleton - 817m - located in the far northwest part of the province]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Prince Edward Island
2\. What are the borders of Prince Edward Island? [A: Northumberland Strait, the Atlantic Ocean]
3\. Turn to p.41. What is the capital? [A: Charlottetown]
4\. How is the island accessed? [A: ferry, Confederation Bridge between New Brunswick and PEI]

➜ MAP WORK
You will continue to label your blank map of Canada in your geography notebook.
1\. Label Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Prince Edward Island.
2\. For each, label the capital, a couple of the major bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. (Optional) If you have additional time, choose one province/territory and write at least 3 things about it using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Take out one of your blank maps of Canada and label as many places as you remember from the provinces and territories studied thus far. Then compare your work to the atlas or your notebook.

➜ VIEW

Canada Mapping - Québec & Ontario
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Québec
2\. What are the water borders of Québec? [A: Hudson Bay, Hudson Strait, Labrador Sea, the Gulf of St. Lawrence, St. Lawrence River]
3\. What are the land borders of Québec? [A: Ontario, Newfoundland and Labrador, New Brunswick, United States of America]
4\. Turn to p.39. What is the capital? [A: Québec City]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mont d'Iberville - 1652m - located in the far northeast part of the province, on the east side of Ungava Bay]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Ontario
2\. What are the borders of Ontario? [A: Manitoba, Quebec, United States of America, Hudson Bay]
3\. What bodies of water form a natural border to the south? [A: The Great Lakes]
4\. What are the names of the Great Lakes [A: Lake Superior, Lake Michigan, Lake Huron, Lake Erie, and Lake Ontario]
5\. Turn to p.38. What is the capital? [A: Toronto]
6\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Ishpatina Ridge - 693m - located to the west of Haileybury, a town on the Québec border]

➜ MAP WORK
You will begin to label your blank map of Canada in your geography notebook.
1\. Label Québec and Ontario.
2\. For both, label the capital, a couple of the major bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. (Optional) If you have additional time, choose one province/territory and write at least 3 things about it using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

Canada Mapping: Manitoba, Saskatchewan, and Alberta
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Manitoba
2\. What are the borders of Manitoba? [A: Ontario, Nunavut, Saskatchewan, United States of America]
3\. Turn to p.37. What is the capital? [A: Winnipeg]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Baldy Mountain - 832m - located in the southwest quadrant, west of Winnipegosis]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Saskatchewan
2\. What are the borders of Saskatchewan? [A: Manitoba, Northwest Territories, Alberta, United States of America]
3\. What do you notice about Saskatchewan's borders? [A: They are all straight lines; the borders are artificial and not created by landforms or bodies of water.]
4\. Turn to p.36. What is the capital? [A: Regina]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Cypress Hills - 1468m - located in the southwest corner of the province]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Alberta
2\. What are the borders of Alberta? [A: Saskatchewan, Northwest Territories, British Columbia, United States of America]
3\. Turn to p.35. What is the capital? [A: Edmonton]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Columbia - 3747m - located on the border with British Columbia between Jasper and Banff]

➜ MAP WORK
You will continue to label your blank map of Canada in your geography notebook.
1\. Label Manitoba, Saskatchewan, and Alberta.
2\. For each, label the capital, a couple of the major bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. (Optional) If you have additional time, choose one province/territory and write at least 3 things about it using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

The Passage at Last
⍞ Materials: Exploring the Frozen North, Canadian Student Atlas

➜ RECAP
It has been several weeks since we left Captain Robert McClure and the Investigator in the ice. What do you recall from the last reading?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.160-168 "McClure was determined" - "never met up with one another."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas, p.73
We rarely look at images of the globe from this perspective. A map like this is called the North Polar Azimuthal Projection, or the North Circumpolar Map, or, more colloquially, the Top of the World Map.
1\. What do you notice about the Arctic when you look down on it directly above the North Pole?
2\. Comparing this map to the map on p.163 of Exploring the Frozen North, can you trace McClure's route with your finger on the North Circumpolar Map?

⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, notebook

➜ DRAW
p.87-88 Map Drawing 30 Antarctica

➜ VIEW
It will be helpful to look at this map of Antarctica as you draw yours.
∞ Map Link: Map of Antarctica

The Rescue of the Investigator
⍞ Materials: Exploring the Frozen North

➜ RECAP
What do you recall from last week's reading about McClure's discovery of the Passage?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.168-177 "Meanwhile, McClure's luck" - "as he claimed he could."

• How did some of McClure's decisions, and his ambition, delay his discovery and rescue by nearby ships?

Final Project: Create a World Map
⍞ Materials: Mapping the World with Art, materials will vary depending on which option you choose. Check your book p.136, for the various possibilities.

PREP: Parents, this should be a final project that requires at least 4 weeks to complete. Make sure to read through the instructions before beginning. Once you and your student have decided which option they will work on, begin to gather the materials needed. Have a designated space where they can put their final project away safely to be worked on the next time. Maps are not perfect; have the student plan out their project in quadrants to ease the process. Ideally, they should be working from memory as best they can. The book recommends that you help them include a few reference points before they begin, such as longitude and/or latitude lines like the Equator, Prime Meridian, or Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, to name a few. (Use light lines for these points, as they are mere references for their map drawings.)

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
p.135 Activity Section: Final Project: Creating a World Map
Create a World Map following the instructions in your book, starting on p.135. Take four weeks to complete, working on one quadrant each week.
Have fun!

Return to England
⍞ Materials: Exploring the Frozen North, Canadian Student Atlas

➜ RECAP
What do you recall about the rescue of Robert McClure and his ship from the last lesson?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.177-186 "Kellett now suggested" - "and John Franklin himself."

• Compare Collinson's journey to McClure's. What are some examples of Collinson missing out on some important discoveries?

• EXTRA HELPING
In today's reading, Berton recalls that the wreck of the HMS Breadalbane was discovered by Canadian divers in 1981. Read more about that wreck and view a painting of the Breadalbane alongside the Phoenix locked in the ice.
∞ Article Link: The world's most northerly shipwreck

What is an Arctic deep-sea dive like? Watch this footage of a dive to one of Franklin's ships, the HMS Terror. Berton, who died in 2004, never lived to see the discovery of the Erebus and Terror, discovered in 2014 and 2016 respectively.
∞ Video Link: Parks Canada Guided Tour Inside HMS Terror

Canada Mapping - Northwest Territories, Yukon, British Columbia
⍞ Materials: Canadian Student Atlas, notebook

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Northwest Territories
2\. What are the borders of the Northwest Territories? [A: Yukon, Beaufort Sea, Nunavut, British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: Yellowknife]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mount Sir James MacBrien - 2762 m - located in the western part of the territory]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: Yukon
2\. What are the borders of Yukon? [A: Northwest Territories, British Columbia, Beaufort Sea, Alaska]
3\. Turn to p.32. What is the capital? [A: Whitehorse]
4\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Logan - 5959m - located in the southwest corner]

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Canadian Student Atlas p.12-13
1\. Locate: British Columbia
2\. What are the borders of British Columbia? [A: Pacific Ocean, Yukon, Alberta, United States of America - Alaska and Washington]
3\. What mountain ranges go through the province? [A: Coast Mountains and Rocky Mountains]
4\. Turn to p.34. What is the capital? [A: Victoria] Where is that located? [A: Vancouver Island]
5\. What is the highest point, and where is that located? [A: Mt. Fairweather - 4663 m - located in the northwest corner]

➜ MAP WORK
You will continue to label your blank map of Canada in your geography notebook.
1\. Label Northwest Territories, Yukon, British Columbia.
2\. For each, label the capital, a couple of the major bodies of water, the highest point, and anything else of interest.
3\. (Optional) If you have additional time, choose one province/territory and write at least 3 things about it using the information on p.14-31. You can tell about the climate, the vegetation, agriculture, industry, population, etc.

Final Project: Create a World Map (cont.)
